item.title: SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 55, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 42, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, code repository, public implementation details, baseline names and absolute scores

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.CL.; Proposes three named stages: CCE, AAE, and TTE.; Evaluates on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA.; Claims cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization tests.

TL;DR: SkillCAT is a training-free framework for LLM agent skill self-evolution that uses contrastive causal evidence from success/failure trajectories, replay-based patch assessment, and topology-aware skill routing to improve benchmark performance.

Problem: The paper studies how LLM agents can convert execution trajectories into reusable skill documents more reliably and efficiently than current skill self-evolution pipelines, which often use only one trajectory per task, merge patches before validation, and load an entire skill corpus at inference time.

Method: SkillCAT separates skill evolution into Contrastive Causal Extraction, which samples multiple trajectories and compares same-task success/failure pairs; Assessment-Augmented Evolution, which replays candidate skill patches on source-task clones before merging; and Topology-Aware Task Execution, which organizes skills into routable sub-skill nodes so inference loads only relevant capabilities.

Key contribution: The novelty claimed in the abstract is the combination of outcome-contrastive causal extraction, pre-merge replay validation of skill patches, and topology-aware inference-time skill retrieval in a training-free LLM-agent skill-evolution framework.

Main result: On SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, DocVQA, plus cross-model and out-of-distribution tests, SkillCAT reportedly improves average score over baselines by up to 40.40%.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify baseline identities, absolute scores, ablations, statistical significance, implementation details, code availability, or failure cases, so the strength and reproducibility of the empirical claim cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matched the keyword 'causality' because its core extraction step is explicitly called Contrastive Causal Extraction and uses success/failure trajectory comparisons to identify evidence explaining outcome differences, though the main application is LLM-agent skill evolution rather than general causal inference.

Tags: LLM agents, skill evolution, causal extraction, contrastive learning, agent benchmarks, training-free framework

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline diagram showing CCE, AAE, hierarchical patch merging, and TTE skill-topology routing from trajectories to inference-time skill loading.

Authors: Kunfeng Chen, Qihuang Zhong, Juhua Liu, Bo Du

item.title: Semiparametric Bayesian inference for causal mediation in cluster randomized trials
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 44, Paper 50, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference acceptance supplied., No code repository or software package mentioned., No public data availability mentioned., No numerical simulation results or tables in supplied abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in stat.ME, stat.AP, and stat.CO.; Abstract identifies a concrete failure mode: asymptotic inference in small-cluster CRT mediation can produce biased variance estimates and invalid confidence intervals.; Method combines parametric Bayesian outcome/mediator models with a similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap using a distance metric between clusters.; Claims simulation studies achieve nominal coverage across diverse scenarios and includes an applied CRT example in Kenya.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a semiparametric Bayesian inference framework for causal mediation analysis in cluster randomized trials with cluster-level mediators and few clusters, using a similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap to improve finite-sample uncertainty quantification.

Problem: Causal mediation analysis in cluster randomized trials is difficult when mediators are measured at the cluster level and the number of clusters is small, because standard asymptotic inference can give biased variance estimates and invalid confidence intervals.

Method: The authors use parametric Bayesian models for the outcome and mediator, then quantify uncertainty with a novel similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap that defines distances between clusters so inference can borrow more information from similar clusters while avoiding fully restrictive parametric uncertainty assumptions.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is the similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap for CRT mediation, designed for small-cluster finite-sample settings and cluster-level mediators.

Main result: Simulation studies reportedly achieve nominal coverage probability across diverse scenarios, and the approach is illustrated on a cluster randomized trial in Kenya.

Limitation: The abstract does not report numerical results, comparator methods, code availability, exact simulation settings, robustness checks, or sensitivity analyses for causal assumptions.

Relevance: This directly matches the causality query because it studies causal mediation, natural direct and indirect effects, and finite-sample causal inference in randomized clustered settings.

Tags: causal mediation, cluster randomized trials, Bayesian inference, Bayesian bootstrap, finite-sample inference, stat.ME

Figure note: A useful image would be a method diagram showing the CRT structure, cluster-level mediator, outcome/mediator Bayesian models, similarity-distance construction between clusters, SWBB resampling, and estimation of natural direct and indirect effects.

Authors: Woojung Bae, Michael Daniels, Joseph Hogan, Rajesh Vedanthan, Stavroula Chrysanthopoulou

item.title: Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories
item.description:
Priority: 62/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 55, Empirical 86, Reproducibility 72, Manuscript 84

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference venue, direct code repository URL, repository quality details for the replication package, ablation details beyond robustness checks

Score evidence: 151 open-source Java repositories, including 74 treated and 77 propensity-matched controls.; 1,811 monthly Arcan snapshots across 13-month per-repository windows.; Uses staggered difference-in-differences with the Borusyak imputation estimator.; Reports robustness checks: wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, and stale-observation sensitivity.

TL;DR: The paper causally studies whether agentic AI coding-tool adoption changes software architectural quality in Java repositories, finding that lower architectural smell density is driven by LOC growth rather than fewer smells.

Problem: Causal evidence is scarce on whether AI coding tools, especially agentic or "vibe coding" workflows, degrade software architecture rather than only code-level metrics.

Method: The authors mine 151 open-source Java repositories, compare 74 repositories with detectable agentic AI adoption against 77 propensity-matched controls, build 1,811 monthly Arcan snapshots, and estimate treatment effects using staggered difference-in-differences with the Borusyak imputation estimator.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is moving causal mining of AI coding-tool adoption from code-level outcomes to architecture-level smell metrics, while explicitly decomposing density changes into raw smell counts and system-size effects.

Main result: Total architectural smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82), LOC increases by 12.8% (p = 0.003), and architectural smell density declines by 6.7% (p = 0.004), indicating a denominator effect rather than a true architectural improvement.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide affiliation, DOI, peer-review status, code URL, repository-quality details, or full dataset-selection validity evidence; adoption detection relies on configuration files and Co-Authored-By trailers, which may miss undocumented AI use.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the `causality` feed because it applies a modern causal inference design, including staggered difference-in-differences, propensity matching, robustness checks, and parallel-trends diagnostics, to software engineering outcomes under AI adoption.

Tags: causal inference, difference-in-differences, software architecture, AI coding tools, agentic AI, software engineering, architectural smells

Figure note: A pipeline figure would be useful showing AI-adoption detection, propensity matching, monthly Arcan snapshots, staggered DiD estimation, and decomposition of architectural smell density into raw smell counts versus LOC.

Authors: Oliver Aleksander Larsen, Mahyar T. Moghaddam

item.title: HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.CV and cs.AI.; Abstract claims HYDRA-X is the first UMM to unify image and video tokenization within a single ViT.; Abstract reports ablations finding frame-level causal temporal attention suffices for reconstruction and hierarchical temporal compression outperforms single-step alternatives.; Abstract claims a lightweight decompressor with joint image-video teacher supervision embeds complementary semantic structures.

TL;DR: HYDRA-X proposes a unified image-video visual tokenizer inside a native ViT for unified multimodal models, with temporal compression and latent-level source-target interaction for improved understanding, generation, and editing.

Problem: The paper studies how to build holistic visual tokenizers for unified multimodal models that can represent both images and videos in one compact latent space while preserving reconstruction quality and semantic awareness.

Method: It introduces HYDRA-X, a 7B dense unified multimodal model using a single ViT-based tokenizer for image and video tokenization, frame-level causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, a lightweight decompressor trained with joint image-video teacher supervision, and a latent-level editing pipeline.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is unifying image and video tokenization within one native ViT and moving source-target editing interaction into the tokenizer latent space rather than relying on semantic-level interaction inside the LLM.

Main result: The abstract reports strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks, and says ablations support frame-level causal temporal attention and hierarchical temporal compression; no numeric scores, datasets, or benchmark names are provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not provide quantitative results, benchmark details, baseline comparisons, code availability, data availability, robustness analysis, or external validation; the relevance to a causality feed is likely only lexical through 'causal temporal attention,' not causal inference or causal reasoning.

Relevance: The matched query is 'causality,' but the paper appears to use 'causal' in the attention-mask/autoregressive temporal sense for visual reconstruction, so it is only weakly relevant unless the feed also tracks causal attention mechanisms in multimodal architectures.

Tags: multimodal models, visual tokenizer, image-video tokenization, Vision Transformer, video generation, image generation, causal temporal attention, editing pipeline

Figure note: A useful image would be the HYDRA-X architecture or pipeline figure showing the unified ViT tokenizer, hierarchical temporal compression, decompressor with teacher supervision, and latent-level editing interaction.

item.title: Causal invariance in graphical models with latent variables
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 50, Empirical 25, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, public data, implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in stat.ME.; Abstract explicitly studies causal discovery with latent parents and causal invariance.; Claims characterization of induced graphs under latent-parent configurations.; Claims necessary and sufficient invariance-testing conditions for a multivariate Gaussian target.

TL;DR: This paper studies when causal invariance can still identify observed causal parents in graphical models where some parent variables are latent, causing the observed-variable graph to lose standard DAG structure or uniqueness.

Problem: Causal invariance methods rely on stable causal effects across settings to identify parents of a target variable, but unobserved parents can induce non-DAG and non-unique observed graphs, making parent identification and invariance testing ambiguous.

Method: The paper characterizes induced graphs under relevant latent-parent configurations and derives formal conditions under which causal invariance is preserved for identifying observed parents, with necessary and sufficient invariance-testing conditions for a multivariate Gaussian target.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution, as supported by the abstract, is a formal treatment of causal invariance under latent-parent structures, including graph characterization and exact testing conditions for Gaussian targets.

Main result: The abstract reports formal necessary and sufficient conditions for testing invariance in the multivariate Gaussian target case, but provides no empirical benchmark results or quantitative performance metrics.

Limitation: Evidence is theoretical from the abstract only; no datasets, baselines, simulations, ablations, robustness checks, code, or empirical validation are mentioned.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal discovery' because it addresses a core failure mode in causal discovery: latent variables breaking DAG assumptions and complicating identification of causal parents.

Tags: causal discovery, causal invariance, latent variables, graphical models, DAGs, Gaussian models, statistical methodology

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or graph diagram contrasting the full latent-variable DAG with the induced observed-variable graph and marking when causal invariance is preserved.

Authors: Marco Borriero, Monia Lupparelli, Giovanni M. Marchetti, Veronica Vinciotti

Query: causal discovery, causal inference, causality

item.title: Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models
item.description:
Priority: 38/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 22, Paper 35, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 20, Manuscript 68

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI, journal, or conference venue, open-source code, public data or benchmark details, model architectures and scales

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11.; Studies reward models trained under helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective settings.; Reports that mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models.; Uses activation-based methods to identify objective-associated neurons.

TL;DR: The paper uses activation-based neuron identification and targeted ablations to study causal interference between helpfulness and harmlessness objectives in RLHF reward models.

Problem: Reward models must support both helpful and harmless behavior, but the abstract says the internal mechanisms behind these objectives and their conflicts are poorly understood.

Method: The authors compare reward models trained for helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed objectives, identify objective-associated neurons using activation-based methods, and test their functional roles through targeted ablations.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a mechanistic account of alignment tension: neurons linked to one objective causally support that objective while often harming the opposing objective, and shared helpfulness/harmlessness neurons appear to have disproportionate behavioral influence.

Main result: Mixed-objective reward models often underperform single-objective models, suggesting objective interference. Targeted ablations indicate that identified neurons causally affect the corresponding alignment objective and can negatively affect the other.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, model architectures, reward-model scale, metrics, baselines, ablation details, statistical robustness, code availability, or whether the findings generalize beyond the studied reward models.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it uses targeted neuron ablations to make causal claims about how reward-model internals support helpfulness and harmlessness objectives.

Tags: RLHF, reward-models, mechanistic-interpretability, causal-ablation, alignment, helpfulness, harmlessness

item.title: NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
item.description:

Score evidence: Published as arXiv v1 on 2026-06-11 in cs.CL and cs.AI.; Abstract identifies two hallucination types: unfaithful content and information omission.; Framework has Element-CoT, Date Selection, and Causal-CoT modules.; Abstract claims evaluation on three TLS benchmarks and human evaluation against state-of-the-art baselines.

TL;DR: NTS-CoT proposes a chain-of-thought framework for reducing hallucinations in LLM-based news timeline summarization, including a causal-reasoning module for date-event summarization.

Problem: The paper studies hallucinations in LLM-based timeline summarization of rapidly evolving online news, focusing on unfaithful content in news summaries and omitted information in date-event summaries.

Method: It proposes NTS-CoT, a three-module framework: Element-CoT extracts essential news elements for faithful summarization, Date Selection combines temporal saliency with event prominence to choose timestamps, and Causal-CoT infers causal relationships to reduce omissions in date-event summarization.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is an explicit hallucination-focused TLS framework that separates hallucination types and uses CoT reasoning, including causal reasoning, to improve faithfulness and event coverage.

Main result: The abstract reports extensive quantitative experiments on three TLS benchmarks plus human evaluation, with NTS-CoT outperforming state-of-the-art baselines and reducing hallucinations; exact metrics and effect sizes are not provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical results, dataset names, ablation details, robustness analysis, model settings, or author/institution/citation metadata. The code link is anonymous, so repository quality and long-term availability are uncertain.

Relevance: The paper matches the configured query "causality" because Causal-CoT explicitly infers causal relationships between events to reduce information omissions in news timeline summarization, though the paper is primarily about summarization faithfulness rather than causal discovery or formal causal inference.

Tags: timeline-summarization, LLM, hallucination-mitigation, chain-of-thought, causal-reasoning, news-summarization, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful image would be the NTS-CoT pipeline diagram showing Element-CoT, Date Selection, and Causal-CoT flow from news articles to timeline summaries.

item.title: When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 45, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation or h-index signal, DOI or venue, citation count, open-source code URL, public data or benchmark details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG.; Abstract reports two same-scale 0.6B Block AttnRes/Qwen3 checkpoints.; Uses identical routing-ablation interventions across a wrapped vanilla Qwen3 baseline and a trained AttnRes checkpoint.; Finds dissociation between average routing mass and causal importance.

TL;DR: The paper tests whether learned cross-layer routing in Block Attention Residuals is actually interpretable by comparing descriptive routing weights with causal ablation effects in two 0.6B Qwen3-based checkpoints.

Problem: It studies whether exposing residual-stream routing as an explicit forward-pass tensor is enough to support mechanistic interpretation of information flow across model depth.

Method: The author applies identical routing-ablation interventions to two same-scale 0.6B checkpoints: a vanilla Qwen3 wrapped with a deterministic recency-bias routing schedule, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing included in optimization.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is the distinction between observable routing mass and causal mechanism: explicit architectural routing makes depth-source paths inspectable, but the paper argues those paths must still be validated with interventions because routing weights alone can be misleading.

Main result: The wrapped baseline shows content-independent routing matching the analytic schedule, while the trained AttnRes model develops three localized routing motifs; however, the largest average routing-mass slice is not the largest causal contributor, and one source family has appreciable mass without detectable causal effect under intervention.

Limitation: The abstract reports only two 0.6B checkpoints and does not mention datasets, downstream metrics, statistical robustness, code release, broader model scales, or independent replications.

Relevance: This is highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the central evidence comes from causal routing-ablation probes and explicitly contrasts correlational routing summaries with causal importance.

Tags: causality, mechanistic-interpretability, transformer-routing, residual-connections, ablation, Qwen3

Figure note: A useful image would show the Block Attention Residual routing tensor across depth sources, the three reported routing motifs, and how routing-ablation interventions compare routing mass with causal contribution.

item.title: MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 35, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count, h-index or author impact signals, DOI, conference or journal venue, open-source code

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11.; Abstract claims fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend.; Evaluated on 2WikiMultihopQA against baseline vLLM.; Reported metrics include 49% prefill throughput improvement, up to two orders of magnitude cached-span TTFT reduction, linear uncached-span scaling, and 5.7% worst-case overhead.

TL;DR: MiniPIC is a small vLLM design for position-independent KV-cache reuse of recurring document/code spans in retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads.

Problem: The paper studies how to reuse KV-cache entries for recurring structured inputs that do not appear as identical prefixes, a case where standard prefix caching in engines like vLLM fails and existing production PIC approaches can require invasive server changes or costly host-device KV transfers.

Method: MiniPIC combines a positional-encoding-free KV cache with user-controlled token-level cache-reuse primitives. It stores unrotated K vectors, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes block-aligned padding, span separator, and prompt depend primitives to control hashing and effective block-level causal attention.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a minimal production-oriented PIC interface for vLLM: fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend are claimed to support multiple PIC methods, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, within one running vLLM instance while integrating with KV-cache CPU offload.

Main result: On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling reportedly improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and has 5.7% worst-case overhead.

Limitation: The abstract reports one named benchmark and headline systems metrics, but does not mention code release, public artifacts, ablations, statistical robustness, broader workload coverage, affiliations, citations, DOI, or peer review status.

Relevance: The match to the configured query 'causality' appears indirect: the paper discusses causal attention structure in inference caching, not causal inference or causal modeling. It is more relevant to LLM serving, RAG systems, and KV-cache optimization than to causality research.

Tags: LLM inference, KV cache, position-independent caching, vLLM, RAG systems, agentic workloads, systems optimization

Figure note: A useful figure would show the MiniPIC pipeline inside vLLM, especially how unrotated K vectors, per-request logical positions, span separators, prompt dependencies, and block-level causal attention interact.

Authors: Nathan Ordonez, Thomas Parnell

Query: causality

item.title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 48, Empirical 74, Reproducibility 68, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, citation count or influential-citation signal, ablation results, statistical significance or confidence intervals

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11.; Abstract evaluates five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets.; Reports average MAE reduction of 4.7% and RMSE reduction of 5.0%.; Code URL is supplied: https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

TL;DR: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for spatio-temporal graph neural networks that learns multi-period temporal, spatial, and cross-period causal patterns to reduce forecasting errors caused by temporal mirages.

Problem: The paper studies spatio-temporal forecasting in urban data, focusing on 'temporal mirage' cases where similar short-window histories lead to divergent futures, or dissimilar histories lead to similar futures, which existing STGNNs are said to miss.

Method: It proposes Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a pre-training plugin for existing STGNN backbones. MP3 uses edge-convolution-based multi-period temporal modeling, bottleneck projection plus a global memory bank for heterogeneous global spatial relations, and a causality-enhanced Transformer for cross-period pattern interaction.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, as described in the abstract, is framing temporal mirages as arising from incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality, then addressing them with a multi-period pre-training module that can be attached to multiple STGNN backbones.

Main result: Across five STGNN baselines and five real-world datasets, including a large-scale CA dataset, MP3 reportedly gives consistent improvements, reducing MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0% on average.

Limitation: The abstract does not report ablations, statistical significance, full dataset details, robustness tests, or peer-review status; code is linked, but repository quality and reproducibility are not verifiable from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: Relevant to the configured query 'causality' because the framework explicitly includes a causality-enhanced Transformer to model cross-period dependencies in spatio-temporal forecasting.

Tags: spatio-temporal forecasting, STGNN, pre-training, causality, time series, urban computing, graph neural networks

Figure note: A useful image would be the MP3 architecture or pipeline figure showing multi-period temporal modeling, multi-period spatial modeling with memory bank, and causality-enhanced Transformer integration with STGNN backbones.

item.title: Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
item.description:
TL;DR: SWITCH makes latent chain-of-thought reasoning trainable with on-policy RL and more causally interpretable by using explicit discrete tokens to enter and exit hidden-state recurrence.

Problem: The paper studies how to make latent chain-of-thought methods based on hidden-state recurrence easier to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning and easier to analyze mechanistically or causally.

Method: It proposes SWITCH, where a model emits <swi> to enter latent reasoning mode and </swi> to exit, trains with a visible-to-latent curriculum, and uses a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation.

Key contribution: The novel idea is to use ordinary discrete boundary tokens as both RL-compatible decision anchors and intervention/probing points for mechanistic analysis of latent reasoning steps.

Main result: According to the abstract, SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale, and boundary-token analysis suggests the entry transition performs problem-specific, causally important computation.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, quantitative gains, model scale details, or whether the mechanistic findings generalize beyond the tested tasks and training setup.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper explicitly studies causal intervention and mechanistic analysis of latent reasoning, including identifying causally important hidden-state transitions.

Tags: latent reasoning, chain-of-thought, reinforcement learning, GRPO, mechanistic interpretability, causal intervention, language models

Figure note: A useful image would be the SWITCH pipeline or architecture diagram showing visible reasoning, <swi>/</swi> boundary tokens, recurrent latent steps, GRPO training, and causal probes at the boundary transitions.

Authors: Jiayu Yang, Chao Chen, Shengen Wu, Yinhong Liu, Yuxuan Fan, Lujundong Li, Songning Lai, Chengwei Qin, Zhijiang Guo

item.title: CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts
item.description:
TL;DR: CausalMoE proposes a billion-scale multimodal Mixture-of-Experts foundation model for Granger causal discovery that routes time-series patches by latent temporal patterns to improve causal graph recovery under regime shifts.

Problem: The paper studies Granger causal discovery for real-world time series where distribution shifts, dynamic regimes, and heterogeneous temporal patterns cause standard neural methods to learn entangled representations and spurious causal graphs.

Method: It proposes CausalMoE, combining pattern-routed heterogeneous experts for patch-level temporal heterogeneity, causality-aware self-attention across variables for sparse graph recovery via proximal optimization, and multimodal alignment with LLM/VLM priors to regularize causal estimation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly routes temporal patches to specialized experts and integrates textual and visual priors, rather than using a single shared neural causal discovery model for all regimes.

Main result: The abstract reports new state-of-the-art results on fully supervised benchmarks and improved generalization in few-shot settings where traditional methods fail, but it does not provide quantitative metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, effect sizes, ablation evidence, computational cost, or whether multimodal priors improve causal validity beyond benchmark performance.

Relevance: This directly matches the configured query 'causal discovery' because it targets Granger causal graph recovery and proposes a new neural framework for causality in temporal systems.

Tags: causal discovery, Granger causality, time series, mixture of experts, foundation model, multimodal learning, few-shot generalization

Figure note: A useful image would be the model pipeline showing patch-level pattern routing, heterogeneous experts, causality-aware variable attention, and LLM/VLM prior alignment for causal graph recovery.

Authors: Bo Liu, Di Dai, Jingwei Liu, Jiarui Jin, Xiaocheng Fang, Guangkun Nie, Hongyan Li, Shenda Hong

item.title: SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 45, Paper 50, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts or h-index signals not supplied., DOI, venue, or peer-review status not supplied., Code repository availability not stated., Public data or benchmark release details not stated., Exact metrics, benchmark size, model list, and statistical robustness not reported in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.AI.; Benchmark covers deduction, induction, and causal abduction.; Tasks are generated from formal objects with verifiable answers.; Difficulty is varied along extraction and inference axes.

TL;DR: SciR is a controllable benchmark for evaluating LLM scientific reasoning across deduction, induction, and causal abduction, with separate difficulty controls for information extraction and formal inference.

Problem: The paper studies how to reliably evaluate LLMs on scientific reasoning when human-annotated scientific benchmarks are expensive and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logic benchmarks often fail to resemble real scientific documents.

Method: SciR generates tasks from formal objects, including deduction trees, inductive rule hypotheses, and causal graphs, then renders them into multi-document scientific discourse using domain-tuned genres. This gives verifiable answers and lets the benchmark independently vary extraction difficulty and inference difficulty.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control over both document-level extraction difficulty and underlying inference difficulty, including causal abduction as one of the reasoning paradigms.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on six models: both extraction and inference difficulty hurt all models, the effects compound, scientific rendering hurts even neurosymbolic solver pipelines, and reasoning models such as deepseek-r1 outperform non-reasoning instruct models mainly on the inference axis.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact datasets, metrics, model list, code/data availability, statistical robustness, or detailed benchmark size, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This matches the configured query 'causality' because one SciR track is explicitly based on causal graphs and causal abduction, making it relevant to evaluating causal reasoning in LLMs within scientific-text settings.

Tags: LLM evaluation, scientific reasoning, causal abduction, causal graphs, benchmark, deduction, induction, multi-document reasoning

Figure note: A useful image would show the SciR pipeline: formal objects for deduction, induction, and causal graphs feeding controllable scientific document rendering, then evaluation across extraction and inference difficulty axes.

item.title: Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers
item.description:

Score evidence: Introduces Frequency Synchronization Degree as a normalized, permutation-tested metric requiring no prior circuit knowledge.; Reports nine modular-addition configurations over primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131} and three seeds.; FSD leads grokking in all nine cases with mean lead +1722 steps and sign-test p approximately 0.004.; Forking at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay is reported to produce monotone earlier grokking with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Frequency Synchronization Degree as an early, circuit-agnostic predictor of grokking in modular-addition transformers and reports causal intervention evidence linking the delay between circuit synchronization and generalization to weight decay.

Problem: It studies why transformer grokking on modular arithmetic occurs suddenly, with emphasis on the timing, causal structure, and controllability of the Fourier circuit thought to support generalization.

Method: The paper introduces Frequency Synchronization Degree, a normalized permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronization that requires no prior circuit knowledge, then evaluates it across modular-addition runs, compares it with a restricted-logit loss baseline, forks training at the FSD-ceiling step while varying weight decay, and performs architecture ablations.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that Fourier-circuit synchronization can be detected before grokking without hand-identifying the circuit, and that the remaining delay to grokking follows a weight-decay-controlled law, providing causal rather than purely correlational evidence.

Main result: Across nine modular-addition configurations, FSD synchronizes 500-3000 steps before grokking with mean lead of 1722 steps and positive lead in all nine cases; forked-training interventions reportedly show earlier grokking monotonically with larger weight decay, with Delta_t approximately proportional to 1/lambda.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to modular addition grokking settings from the abstract; no code, public artifacts, affiliations, citation signals, robustness beyond the stated primes/seeds, or broader task transfer are provided in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it claims direct causal evidence about how regularization controls the timing between Fourier-circuit synchronization and generalization in grokking transformers.

item.title: Order Is Not Control
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 45, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference status, citation or influential-citation counts, open-source code, public data or benchmark identifiers

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG, cs.AI, and cs.CL.; Abstract reports LLM component-sign accuracy of 72.8-73.7%, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components.; Abstract reports held-out observer prediction accuracies of 93.6% and 91.7%.; Abstract explicitly states several limits: no deployable pre-generation control, no hidden/logit causal sufficiency, no biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and no literal thermodynamic quantities.

TL;DR: The paper argues that order-inducing interventions are not sufficient for control and proposes a receiver-gated response-operator framework for identifying local admitted control across biological systems, LLMs, adapters, and stochastic-operator settings.

Problem: It studies how to distinguish mere order, alignment, steering, interpretability structure, or perturbation response from actual control in systems where interventions can have medium-, bath-, receiver-, and comparator-dependent effects.

Method: The paper proposes a denominator-indexed, receiver-gated response law: an operator mapping material state, action or drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. It evaluates this framing across biological panels, LLM generated-output panels, constitution-conditioned adapters, and stochastic-operator panels.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a control criterion based on local receiver-gated response laws rather than coordinate identity, order induction, or controller attribution: control is assigned only when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive behavior, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort remain bounded.

Main result: From the abstract, LLM response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, increasing to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Biological panels are described as supporting physical response-operator evidence, but detailed sample sizes, datasets, and controls are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract explicitly leaves deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope. It also does not provide code, data, baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, sample sizes, or venue/peer-review signals in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it proposes criteria for causal control rather than correlation/order: interventions are evaluated by receiver-gated response laws, finite-effort target movement, bounded failure modes, and separations between measured opportunity and deployable action policies.

Tags: causality, control, AI alignment, interpretability, LLM steering, response operators, stochastic operators, biological systems

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the denominator-indexed response operator: material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state flowing into response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection, with bounded failure modes used to decide local admitted control.

TL;DR: MAStrike proposes a Shapley-guided closed-loop framework for finding collusive adversarial coalitions in hierarchical multi-agent systems and red-teaming them with coordinated role-aware manipulations.

Problem: The paper studies safety and security failures in hierarchical multi-agent systems where attacks can be distributed across role-specialized agents through privilege escalation, collusion, and coordinated bypasses of system defenses.

Method: It proposes MAStrike, a closed-loop collusive red-teaming framework that estimates agent-level Shapley values for robustness, uses those attributions to identify vulnerable coalitions, generates coordinated role-aware adversarial manipulations, and iteratively refines attacks using structured causal diagnosis of failed attempts.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed is agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS robustness combined with coalition-guided, causal-diagnosis-driven adversarial attack generation, rather than heuristic target selection or isolated message perturbations.

Main result: The abstract reports extensive experiments on hierarchical MAS across finance, software engineering, and CRM, using multiple frontier models, where MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines; no quantitative effect sizes or metric values are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical results, benchmark details, code or data availability, exact baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, or evidence about scalability of Shapley estimation.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because MAStrike uses structured causal diagnosis to attribute attack failures to uncompromised agents and refine collusive red-team strategies in multi-agent systems.

Tags: multi-agent systems, red-teaming, AI security, Shapley values, causal diagnosis, collusion, hierarchical agents

Figure note: A useful image would show the MAStrike pipeline: Shapley-based agent attribution, vulnerable coalition selection, coordinated adversarial manipulation, causal diagnosis of blocked attacks, and iterative refinement across a hierarchical MAS topology.

item.title: Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG.; Abstract claims first causal mechanistic analysis of TabPFN 2.5.; Methods named in abstract: activation patching, ablation, attention entropy, and contrastive activation steering.; Empirical setting visible in abstract: two synthetic regression datasets.

TL;DR: The paper applies causal mechanistic interpretability tools to TabPFN 2.5 and reports that feature-wise attention computation is temporally localized, with one dominant head carrying substantially more causal importance than the others on synthetic regression tasks.

Problem: The paper studies where and how computation is distributed across feature-wise attention heads and layers inside the tabular foundation model TabPFN 2.5, with an emphasis on causal localization rather than purely correlational attention inspection.

Method: It uses activation patching, head ablation, and attention-entropy analysis on two synthetic regression datasets, and additionally tests inference-time contrastive activation steering.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, identifying task-dependent temporal specialization of attention-head function and contrasting TabPFN's context-dependent computation with steering behavior commonly studied in language models.

Main result: From the abstract: one attention head is causally dominant by about 2x to 5x at its peak layer, the dominant layer shifts with task complexity, and attention entropy aligns with patching evidence for the active layers. Contrastive activation steering does not transfer across samples.

Limitation: Evidence is limited in the supplied abstract to two synthetic regression datasets; there is no visible claim about real-world tabular benchmarks, statistical robustness, open-source code, public data, broader ablations, or external validation.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causality' because it uses causal mechanistic methods, including activation patching and ablation, to localize functional components inside a foundation model.

item.description:

Score evidence: Abstract states the setting includes labeled outcomes/regressors plus unlabeled auxiliary regressors.; Abstract derives an efficient influence function and efficiency bound for PPCI.; Abstract proposes EE-DML-PPCI and TMLE-DML-PPCI estimators.; Abstract claims both estimators match the derived efficiency bound.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a semi-supervised, efficiency-theoretic framework for causal and structural parameter estimation that uses unlabeled auxiliary regressors to reduce asymptotic variance relative to labeled-data-only estimators.

Problem: The study addresses semiparametric efficient estimation of causal and structural parameters when labeled observations include outcomes and regressors, while additional unlabeled auxiliary regressors are also available.

Method: It introduces prediction-powered causal inference (PPCI), derives the efficient influence function and efficiency bound, and combines that influence-function construction with debiased machine learning to define EE-DML-PPCI and TMLE-DML-PPCI estimators. It also develops semi-supervised generalized Riesz regression for estimating the Riesz representer with convergence guarantees.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that unlabeled auxiliary regressors can be incorporated into causal/structural parameter estimation to attain a smaller asymptotic variance than the labeled-data-only efficiency bound, with DML/TMLE-style estimators matching the derived semi-supervised efficiency bound.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: both proposed estimators are claimed to have asymptotic variances matching the newly derived efficiency bound. No finite-sample empirical result is reported in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data experiments, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code, data, DOI, venue, or affiliation/citation metadata, so the practical magnitude of the efficiency gain and reproducibility are unclear from the supplied information.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query "causal inference" and is especially relevant to researchers interested in semiparametric efficiency, debiased machine learning, TMLE, Riesz representers, and semi-supervised causal estimation.

Tags: causal inference, semiparametric efficiency, debiased machine learning, TMLE, semi-supervised learning, Riesz regression, prediction-powered inference

item.title: ShrinkageTrees: An R Package for Bayesian Tree Ensembles for Survival Analysis and Causal Inference
item.description:
TL;DR: ShrinkageTrees introduces an R package implementing regularized Bayesian additive regression tree ensembles for censored survival outcomes and causal effect estimation.

Problem: The paper addresses flexible survival modeling and causal inference when outcomes are right- or interval-censored, especially in high-dimensional covariate settings where standard Bayesian tree ensembles may overfit or lack suitable regularization.

Method: It implements Bayesian additive regression tree models in an accelerated failure time framework, with optional decomposition into prognostic and treatment-effect components, using depth-penalizing and Dirichlet splitting priors for tree structure plus global-local shrinkage priors for step heights.

Key contribution: The abstract claims the first implementation of the Horseshoe Forest, which applies a horseshoe prior to tree step heights, and packages this with survival and causal inference workflows in R.

Main result: No empirical benchmark or numerical result is reported in the abstract; the stated result is software capability, including fitting, prediction, causal effect estimation, convergence diagnostics, Rcpp acceleration, and multi-chain MCMC.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide evidence on accuracy, calibration, runtime, scalability, or comparisons against existing survival or causal inference methods.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it provides Bayesian tree ensemble tools for estimating treatment effects, particularly with censored survival outcomes and high-dimensional covariates.

Tags: causal inference, survival analysis, Bayesian additive regression trees, R package, censored outcomes, Horseshoe Forest, MCMC

item.title: The Impossibility of Eliciting Latent Knowledge
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper formalizes eliciting latent knowledge in AI systems using Causal Influence Diagrams and proves that behavior-only feedback cannot guarantee an honest agent, even with perfect training feedback.

Problem: It studies how to train advanced AI systems to honestly report their beliefs about latent environmental variables that humans cannot directly observe, a problem known as eliciting latent knowledge (ELK).

Method: The authors use Causal Influence Diagrams to model the relationship between an agent's training environment, its subjective world representation, observable variables, latent variables, honesty, and goal misgeneralisation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a formal CID-based treatment of ELK that distinguishes honest reporting from merely producing answers humans would judge as true, plus an impossibility theorem for behavior-only feedback strategies.

Main result: The abstract states an impossibility theorem: no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behavior can guarantee production of an honest agent with certainty, even when training feedback is perfect.

Limitation: The abstract does not report empirical experiments, benchmarks, or practical mitigation evidence; the result appears theoretical and applies under the paper's stated formal assumptions.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper uses Causal Influence Diagrams as the core formal framework for reasoning about hidden variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and causal structure in AI alignment.

Tags: causality, causal influence diagrams, AI alignment, eliciting latent knowledge, honesty, goal misgeneralisation, theoretical AI safety

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's CID framework diagram showing observable variables, latent variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and behavior in the training environment.

item.title: Making Foresight Actionable: Repurposing Representation Alignment in World Action Models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies why visually plausible World Action Model rollouts can still yield poor robot actions and proposes AGRA, a representation-alignment objective that makes video diffusion features more useful for action decoding.

Problem: World Action Models for robot manipulation use video generation to predict future scene evolution before choosing actions, but the abstract reports that plausible generated futures do not reliably produce accurate low-level control actions.

Method: The authors diagnose the issue with action-head attention analysis and causal interventions, then introduce AGRA: an Action-Grounded Representation Alignment objective that aligns intermediate video diffusion features with spatially coherent semantic representations from a foundation visual encoder.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is reframing the world-model-to-action interface as a representation mismatch problem: hidden states optimized for visual reconstruction are regularized to become spatially and semantically organized for action control, rather than assuming better video prediction directly implies better actions.

Main result: On real-world manipulation tasks, AGRA reportedly improves attention to task-relevant interaction regions, object localization, affordance understanding, robustness to task-irrelevant perturbations, in-distribution performance, and out-of-distribution generalization over a baseline World Action Model.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, task suite details, baseline names beyond a baseline WAM, or evidence about computational cost and scalability across robot platforms.

Relevance: This is relevant to the `causality` feed because the paper explicitly uses causal interventions to identify why action decoders fail under task-irrelevant perturbations, though the main contribution is representation alignment for robot world-action models rather than a general causal inference method.

Tags: robot manipulation, world action models, video diffusion, representation alignment, causal interventions, attention analysis, affordance learning, out-of-distribution generalization

Figure note: A useful image would show the AGRA pipeline: video diffusion world-model features feeding an action decoder, the semantic foundation-encoder alignment objective, and attention or causal-intervention maps highlighting task-relevant versus task-irrelevant regions.

item.title: Attention by Synchronization in Coupled Oscillator Networks
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes replacing softmax attention with a Kuramoto synchronization-based oscillator mechanism designed for energy-constrained physical hardware.

Problem: It studies how to implement transformer-style attention on physical substrates where exponentiation and global reductions used by softmax are energetically expensive and physically unnatural.

Method: The proposed fixed-query oscillator attention uses learned query anchors fixed on a sphere while free oscillators evolve under Kuramoto-Lohe dynamics until equilibrium; attention weights are then encoded through cosine similarity with an affine normalization at readout.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a mathematically grounded attention operation implemented by oscillator synchronization dynamics, with a provably unique and almost globally attractive fixed point across physical realizations, avoiding exponentiation during computation.

Main result: Empirically, at oscillator dimension 2 it outperforms softmax on keyword spotting by +1.00 percentage point and subject-verb agreement by +5.27 percentage points on hard sentences; on causal language modeling, softmax remains better, but the perplexity gap shrinks as oscillator dimension increases.

Limitation: The abstract frames the work mainly as a hardware blueprint rather than a software replacement for softmax, and reports remaining gaps on causal language modeling; it does not provide hardware energy measurements or physical-device experiments in the abstract.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query 'causality' appears weak and indirect: the abstract mentions causal language modeling, but the paper is about physical implementations of attention, not causal inference or causality methods.

Tags: oscillator-attention, kuramoto-dynamics, physical-ai-hardware, transformer-attention, energy-efficient-computing, neuromorphic-computing

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing fixed query anchors on the sphere, free oscillator equilibration under Kuramoto-Lohe dynamics, and readout of attention weights via cosine similarity.

item.title: WorldReasoner: Evaluating Whether Language Model Agents Forecast Events with Valid Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: WorldReasoner is a benchmark framework for testing whether language-model agents can make temporally valid event forecasts using grounded evidence and causal reasoning rather than memorized facts or unsupported explanations.

Problem: The paper studies how to evaluate real-world event forecasting by language-model agents under uncertainty, especially when final-answer accuracy alone cannot distinguish valid forecasting from memorization, fabricated citations, or post hoc causal storytelling.

Method: It proposes WorldReasoner, an evaluation framework where each task provides a resolved forecasting question, a simulated forecast date, and access only to pre-date evidence; agents submit probabilities, cited evidence, and optionally causal event graphs, which are scored on outcome quality, evidence quality, and reasoning quality against hindsight reference graphs.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is a temporally constrained forecasting benchmark that evaluates not just prediction accuracy but also source grounding and causal-event reasoning, built at scale through an agentic pipeline that generates questions, gathers timestamped evidence, and constructs hindsight event graphs.

Main result: On 345 resolved tasks derived from 14,141 articles and 8,087 extracted graph events, temporally valid retrieval was the strongest driver of forecast accuracy; causal graph construction improved key-event recovery, but agents still struggled to turn grounded evidence into well-calibrated probabilities.

Limitation: The abstract does not report detailed model identities, statistical significance, human validation quality for the hindsight graphs, or whether results generalize beyond the 345 constructed forecasting tasks.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because it evaluates whether LM agents can use causal event graphs and key-event structure to support real-world forecasts, rather than relying only on surface evidence or outcome accuracy.

Tags: causality, event forecasting, language model agents, evaluation benchmark, temporal validity, retrieval, causal event graphs

Figure note: A useful image would show the WorldReasoner evaluation pipeline: forecasting question generation, timestamped evidence retrieval, agent probability and citation submission, optional causal graph construction, and the three scoring axes.

item.title: AnchorEdit: Maintaining Temporal Consistency in Multi-turn Image Editing via Causal Memory
item.description:
TL;DR: AnchorEdit proposes an autoregressive diffusion framework with causal memory to reduce identity drift and accumulated errors in long-horizon multi-turn image editing.

Problem: The paper studies high-resolution iterative image editing, where models must preserve subject identity and follow instructions over many successive edits but often suffer from identity drift and error accumulation.

Method: It proposes AnchorEdit, an autoregressive diffusion-based multi-turn editing framework trained with a three-stage curriculum: identity-preserving single-turn pretraining, causal autoregressive forcing fine-tuning using self-rollout to reduce exposure bias, and consistency distillation for efficient 4-step generation; at inference, a memory mechanism anchors the initial subject identity across editing rounds.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is reframing multi-turn image editing as causal autoregressive inference rather than relying on bidirectional video-prior attention, plus adding a causal memory mechanism and a high-resolution benchmark for long-horizon editing stability.

Main result: According to the abstract, AnchorEdit achieves state-of-the-art results, preserving subject fidelity and instruction following over more than 10 interaction rounds.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative metrics, dataset details, baseline names, ablations, or evidence about generalization beyond the proposed benchmark.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' mainly through its use of causal autoregressive inference and memory for sequential generation; it is not clearly about causal effect estimation or causal discovery.

Tags: multi-turn image editing, autoregressive diffusion, causal memory, identity preservation, long-horizon consistency, computer vision

Figure note: A useful image would be the AnchorEdit pipeline or architecture figure showing the three-stage training curriculum and inference-time causal memory mechanism.

item.title: From Prompts to Tokens: Internalizing Causal Supervision in Vision-Language Model for Multi-Image Causal Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: BridgeVLM proposes to improve multi-image visual causal reasoning by turning induced causal graphs into internal causal tokens processed inside a VLM decoder rather than relying on prompt-level causal instructions.

Problem: The paper studies brittle causal reasoning in large vision-language models, especially identifying causal variables from multi-image visual inputs and answering interventional or counterfactual queries about physical scenes.

Method: It proposes BridgeVLM, which induces a causal graph from multi-image inputs, converts that graph into structured Causal Tokens, and executes them through RAMP layers inserted into the LLM decoder for causal message passing; it also introduces M3S, a unified training interface for local and global causal supervision.

Key contribution: The main novelty is internalizing causal supervision into model execution via causal tokens and decoder-level message passing, instead of keeping causal knowledge external as text prompts.

Main result: From the abstract, BridgeVLM reports 54.4% accuracy on CausalVLBench intervention tasks versus 33.2% with prompt-level supervision, improves Causal3D from 43.6% to 49.0%, and raises CausalVLBench causal structure learning F1 from 33.4% to 75.1%.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify model scale, dataset breadth beyond CausalVLBench and Causal3D, robustness to real-world visual distributions, computational overhead from RAMP layers, or whether improvements generalize outside benchmark-style causal tasks.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because it targets visual causal reasoning, causal structure learning, interventions, and counterfactual reasoning in VLMs.

Tags: visual causal reasoning, vision-language models, causal structure learning, interventions, counterfactual reasoning, multi-image reasoning

Figure note: A useful image would be the BridgeVLM architecture or pipeline figure showing multi-image inputs, induced causal graph, Causal Token construction, and RAMP-layer message passing inside the LLM decoder.

item.title: Bracketing Relationships of Weighted Average Treatment Effects
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper derives theoretical bracketing relationships among weighted average treatment effects in observational causal inference, especially showing when the overlap-weight estimand lies between ATT and ATC.

Problem: It studies how different weighted average treatment effect estimands relate to each other in observational studies, where treatment effect heterogeneity and propensity-score-based weighting can make causal estimands difficult to compare.

Method: The authors prove theoretical bounds under a monotonic relationship between the propensity score and the conditional average treatment effect, then extend the argument to weighted local average treatment effects with a binary instrumental variable and binary treatment, as well as to other weighting schemes.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a bracketing result: under stated monotonicity conditions, the overlap-weight average treatment effect is bounded between the average treatment effects on treated and control units, giving an interpretable ordering of common causal estimands.

Main result: From the abstract, the primary result is theoretical rather than empirical: the overlap-weight estimand is bounded between ATT and ATC under the monotonic propensity-score/CATE relationship, with analogous extensions to weighted LATE settings.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data validation, or robustness evidence; the results appear to depend on monotonicity assumptions whose empirical plausibility may need diagnostic assessment.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it clarifies relationships among treatment-effect estimands used in observational causal studies and proposes a CP-plot diagnostic of estimated CATE versus estimated propensity score.

Tags: causal inference, observational studies, weighted average treatment effects, overlap weights, ATT, ATC, LATE, propensity score

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or diagnostic figure showing the proposed CP-plot: estimated conditional average treatment effect on one axis and estimated propensity score on the other, with regions illustrating the bracketing relationship among ATT, ATC, and overlap-weight ATE.

item.title: Substrate Asymmetry in User-Side Memory: A Diagnostic Framework
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper argues that user-side LLM memory should be evaluated along separate behavioral and factual axes because parametric user adapters and retrieval systems fail in opposite ways.

Problem: It studies how to evaluate and choose memory substrates for personalized LLMs, showing that aggregate personalization scores obscure distinct failures in style consistency, factual recall, and abstention when facts are absent.

Method: The paper proposes a diagnostic framework that factorizes memory into behavioral consistency, factual presence, and factual absence, then compares per-user gamma-LoRA adapters against BGE-large top-K retrieval on a controlled 50-user synthetic corpus and a LaMP-3 real-data probe, with causal intervention by zeroing specific LoRA attention weights.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is identifying substrate asymmetry: LoRA-based parametric memory and RAG specialize in different memory axes, and the same query-projection attention cells in layers 21-35 appear causally load-bearing for opposite factual-presence and factual-absence effects.

Main result: Gamma-LoRA decisively improves behavioral style, while RAG decisively improves factual absence. Zeroing the identified LoRA weights raises absence-probe TPR by 33 percentage points but drops presence-probe TPR by 20 points. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, alignment appears to worsen the asymmetry; on LaMP-3, gamma-LoRA underperforms a majority baseline unless evaluation-time logit masking is used.

Limitation: The abstract reports a controlled synthetic corpus of only 50 users and a LaMP-3 probe; broader real-world validation, robustness across more tasks/models, and details of the causal intervention methodology are not available from the abstract.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper makes causal claims about specific LoRA attention query-projection weights load-bearing for memory behavior, using weight-zeroing interventions to test their effects.

Tags: LLM memory, personalization, LoRA, RAG, causal intervention, attention analysis, evaluation framework, alignment tax

Figure note: A useful image would show the diagnostic framework with the three memory axes, the compared substrates, and the causal intervention on attention query-projection cells in layers 21-35.

item.title: Organize then Retrieve: Hierarchical Memory Navigation for Efficient Agents
item.description:
TL;DR: HORMA proposes a hierarchical file-system-like working memory for LLM agents that preserves raw trajectories while enabling efficient navigation-based retrieval for long-horizon tasks.

Problem: The paper studies how LLM agents can maintain useful working memory over long-horizon, multi-step tasks without relying on ever-growing contexts that degrade reasoning quality, increase cost, and add latency.

Method: HORMA builds a hierarchical memory structure from agent experience, linking summarized entities to raw trajectories, then uses a lightweight reinforcement-learning-trained navigation agent to traverse the hierarchy and retrieve minimal sufficient context for the current task.

Key contribution: The novelty is an organize-then-retrieve memory framework that explicitly preserves temporal structure and causal dependencies by connecting summaries to raw experience, instead of depending only on lossy compression or similarity-based retrieval.

Main result: From the abstract, HORMA improves task performance under constrained context budgets across ALFWorld, LoCoMo, and LongMemEval, using at most 22.17% of baseline token usage in long conversation tasks while improving efficiency-performance trade-offs.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify exact accuracy gains, ablation results, training cost for the retrieval agent, or whether the method scales to very large real-world agent histories beyond the evaluated benchmarks.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it targets memory retrieval methods that preserve temporal structure and causal dependencies needed for multi-step agent behavior.

Tags: LLM agents, working memory, hierarchical retrieval, long-horizon tasks, causal dependencies, reinforcement learning, agent memory

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or architecture diagram showing HORMA's two stages: hierarchical memory construction linking summaries to raw trajectories, followed by navigation-based retrieval through the memory tree.

Authors: Hao-Lun Hsu, Nikki Lijing Kuang, Boyi Liu, Zhewei Yao, Yuxiong He

TL;DR: This arXiv v1 survey organizes recent work on LLM reasoning into a broad taxonomy and highlights recurring failure modes, including weak causal abstraction, but it is only tangentially aligned with a causality-focused feed.

Problem: The paper studies why reliable reasoning in LLMs remains difficult despite progress in structured inference, multi-step problem solving, and contextual understanding.

Method: It presents a systematic survey of more than 300 recent papers from arXiv, Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar, Papers with Code, and ACL Anthology, organized into a taxonomy of reasoning paradigms and methodological trends.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is a unified structured taxonomy spanning CoT, multi-hop, mathematical, commonsense, visual, temporal, code, retrieval-augmented, tool-augmented, agentic, and reinforcement-learning-based reasoning, together with synthesized failure modes and future directions.

Main result: No new empirical result is reported in the abstract; the main outcome is a literature synthesis identifying common limitations such as reasoning hallucinations, brittle multi-step inference, weak causal abstraction, and poor cross-domain generalization.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention quantitative survey methodology, selection criteria, inter-annotator agreement, benchmark reanalysis, code/data release, ablations, or robustness checks.

item.title: Bayesian Causal Machine Learning for Cure Models
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 42, Paper 40, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation and h-index information not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No code repository or implementation details mentioned in the abstract., No public data or reproducibility package mentioned., No quantitative simulation metrics reported in the abstract.

Score evidence: Defines causal effects for survival settings with a cured subpopulation.; Proposes BartCure, a Bayesian causal machine learning approach.; Decomposes RMST causal effects into stochastic cure and stochastic latency components.; Reports simulations and an application to the CALGB 40101 breast cancer trial.

TL;DR: The paper introduces BartCure, a Bayesian causal machine learning approach for decomposing treatment effects in survival settings with a cured subpopulation into cure-probability and latency mechanisms.

Problem: The paper studies causal effect estimation in survival studies where treatment may work either by increasing the probability of cure or by delaying failure among patients who are not cured, including whether these mechanisms vary across subpopulations.

Method: It defines causal estimands for cure-model settings and proposes BartCure, a Bayesian causal machine learning method to estimate average and subgroup effects, including a decomposition of restricted mean survival time effects into stochastic cure and stochastic latency components.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a causal framework for cure models that separates treatment benefit into cure and latency mechanisms, connecting these effects to stochastic intervention effects and principal-strata causal effects rather than only estimating finite-horizon survival or RMST.

Main result: From the abstract, simulations show BartCure is competitive for average-effect estimation and especially effective at conservatively detecting the direction of treatment-effect heterogeneity; the method is also applied to the CALGB 40101 breast cancer trial.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative metrics, comparison baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, or detailed dataset coverage, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be fully assessed.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it proposes new causal estimands and a causal machine learning method for survival analysis with cure structures and heterogeneous treatment effects.

Tags: causal inference, survival analysis, cure models, Bayesian machine learning, heterogeneous treatment effects, restricted mean survival time

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing how BartCure separates treatment effects into cure-probability and latency components and how these combine into RMST effects.
